narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average, along with an LSAT score of 33, your middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 46% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, far below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school lagged significantly behind most peers who pass the bar. This stood out as the most influential concern, as it strongly outweighed other positives in the model's eyes. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA had the second-strongest positive pull, pushing the prediction toward bar passage because it's notably higher than average and a key indicator of sustained academic strength. Your LSAT score came in third, also helping your case by nudging things toward success, even if it's a bit below the typical 36.7 seen in passers. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile shows real strengths in prior college work but a concerning dip in later school rankings that mirrors patterns in those who struggle on the bar. While full-time status aligns with the vast majority who succeed, it wasn't a top driver here. This rejection doesn't define your potential—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience as a Hispanic student overcoming any barriers. Reapplying with those improvements could flip the model's view, and many schools value holistic reviews beyond predictions. Keep pushing; your solid foundation positions you well for another shot.
